advantages: A common vocabulary for comparing algorithms regardless of language or hardware.
Lets you predict scaling behavior from code shape — no profiling needed for the first pass.
Identifies asymptotic winners early, before you commit engineering effort.
Surfaces hidden costs (string concatenation, list scans) that look harmless in small tests.
disadvantages: Hides constants — a slower-class algorithm can win on real input sizes.
Says nothing about memory locality, cache behavior or vectorization, all of which dominate in practice.
Worst-case bounds can be pessimistic for typical inputs (e.g. quicksort).
Easy to misapply: ignoring amortization, miscounting nested loops, or forgetting hidden library complexity.
